tooLow: Very low fiber often means harder, less frequent bowel movements, blood sugar swings after meals, and weaker gut barrier function.
Over years, chronically low fiber is linked with higher LDL cholesterol, diverticular disease, and colorectal cancer risk in population studies.
enough: Adequate total fiber (soluble + insoluble) feeds beneficial gut microbes, steadies digestion, and helps you feel full on fewer calories.
Fiber (total) tracks your daily target; soluble (~30%) and insoluble (~70%) targets add up to that total.
tooHigh: Sudden large increases in fiber without enough water can cause bloating, gas, and cramping—raise intake gradually.
Very high fiber with poor hydration may worsen constipation in some people; spread intake across meals.
male: Higher targets (about 38 g/day total) match larger average calorie needs and support heart health and weight management.
Helps reduce diverticular disease risk and supports stable energy when eating higher-protein or higher-fat meals.
female: Adequate fiber supports hormone clearance via the gut and can ease constipation common with cycle shifts or pregnancy.
Helps fullness and blood-sugar stability, which many women find useful for energy and cravings across the month.
bowelMovementsAltered: Fiber is the first-line dietary lever for bowel regularity—too little often means hard, infrequent stools; gradual increases with water soften and bulk stool without harsh laxatives.
Sudden large fiber jumps can temporarily cause bloating, gas, or loose stools—raise intake slowly when trying to fix altered bowel movements.

tooLow: Low soluble fiber means less gel-forming bulk in the gut, which can blunt blood sugar less after meals and feed fewer beneficial gut bacteria.
Over time, low soluble fiber is linked with higher LDL cholesterol in population studies.
enough: Soluble fiber (oats, beans, barley, apples, citrus) forms a gel that slows digestion, lowers LDL cholesterol, and feeds gut microbes that produce protective short-chain fatty acids.
Your soluble target is about 30% of total fiber (~11 g for men, ~8 g for women).
tooHigh: Sudden large increases in soluble fiber without enough water can cause bloating and gas—raise intake gradually.
Very high soluble fiber alone without enough insoluble bulk may not fully support colon transit time.
male: Men benefit from soluble fiber for LDL and blood-sugar support alongside higher calorie intake.
Pair with insoluble sources for full colon and metabolic coverage.
female: Soluble fiber supports hormone clearance via the gut and blood-sugar stability across the cycle.
Oats, beans, and fruit are practical daily sources.
bowelMovementsAltered: Soluble fiber softens stool by absorbing water—helpful when stools are hard, but raise intake gradually.
Works best combined with insoluble fiber and adequate hydration.

tooLow: Low insoluble fiber means less bulk in stool, which can slow transit and contribute to constipation.
Over years, low insoluble fiber with low total fiber is linked with higher colorectal cancer and diverticular disease risk.
enough: Insoluble fiber (whole grains, wheat bran, vegetables, fruit skins) adds bulk, shortens colon transit time, and supports regular bowel movements.
Your insoluble target is about 70% of total fiber (~27 g for men, ~18 g for women).
tooHigh: Sudden large increases in insoluble fiber without enough water can cause cramping and bloating.
Very high bran intake without gradual adaptation may temporarily worsen symptoms.
male: Insoluble fiber shortens colon transit time—a key lever for colon health at higher calorie intake.
Whole grains and vegetables are reliable sources.
female: Insoluble fiber adds bulk that supports regularity, especially helpful during cycle-related constipation.
Whole grains and vegetables should make up much of daily intake.
bowelMovementsAltered: Insoluble fiber adds bulk and speeds transit—first-line support when stools are infrequent or hard.
Increase gradually with water to avoid gas or cramping.

warning: The optimal daily dietary fiber intake ranges between 25 and 38 grams. Experts generally recommend an insoluble-to-soluble fiber ratio of about 2:1 or 3:1—two commonly agreed targets alongside each other. Use one that fits well with your gut (based on how your gut reacts). For a daily target of 30 grams at 2:1, that means roughly 20 grams insoluble fiber and 10 grams soluble fiber.

This 2:1 ratio assumes relatively stable bowel movements. If you currently have constipation or diarrhea—including after a stomach bug or in post-infectious IBS—prioritize the fiber type that fits your symptoms (more insoluble for constipation, more soluble for diarrhea) and ignore this ratio until things settle.
dashboardTracking: This row scores how close your average insoluble-to-soluble fiber ratio is to 2:1. % of target is highest at exactly 2:1 and falls as the ratio moves higher or lower.

warning: The optimal daily dietary fiber intake ranges between 25 and 38 grams. Experts generally recommend an insoluble-to-soluble fiber ratio of about 2:1 or 3:1—two commonly agreed targets alongside each other. Use one that fits well with your gut (based on how your gut reacts). For a daily target of 30 grams at 3:1, that means roughly 22.5 grams insoluble fiber and 7.5 grams soluble fiber.

This 3:1 ratio assumes relatively stable bowel movements. If you currently have constipation or diarrhea—including after a stomach bug or in post-infectious IBS—prioritize the fiber type that fits your symptoms (more insoluble for constipation, more soluble for diarrhea) and ignore this ratio until things settle.
dashboardTracking: This row scores how close your average insoluble-to-soluble fiber ratio is to 3:1. % of target is highest at exactly 3:1 and falls as the ratio moves higher or lower.

tooLow: Too little sodium is uncommon from food alone but can happen with heavy sweating, illness, or very restrictive diets—leading to headache, fatigue, muscle cramps, and dizziness.
Severe hyponatremia affects fluid balance and, in extreme cases, confusion or seizures (usually a medical issue, not mild under-eating).
enough: Enough sodium maintains fluid balance, nerve signals, and muscle contraction—especially when you sweat or train hard.
Staying near guideline levels (about 2,300 mg/day for most adults) helps keep blood pressure in a healthy range and lowers long-term stroke and kidney disease risk.
tooHigh: Chronic high sodium raises blood pressure in many people, which over decades increases heart attack, stroke, heart failure, and kidney damage risk.
Most excess comes from restaurant meals, packaged foods, and cured meats—not the salt shaker alone.
male: Men often eat more sodium through larger portions and processed foods; tracking helps if blood pressure runs high.
Athletes and heavy sweaters may need more sodium around workouts without blowing daily averages every other day.
female: Some women are more salt-sensitive for blood pressure; pairing adequate potassium and whole foods helps balance intake.
Pregnancy, hot flashes, or intense training can change sweat losses—awareness matters more than blanket ultra-low-salt rules.

tooLow: Low potassium can cause muscle weakness, cramps, constipation, and palpitations; severe deficiency is dangerous and needs medical care.
Diuretics, heavy sweating, vomiting, or very low fruit/vegetable intake are common contributors.
enough: Potassium helps nerves and muscles work, supports normal blood pressure, and balances sodium—lowering stroke and kidney stone risk when intake is adequate.
Food sources are preferable to supplements unless a clinician directs otherwise.
tooHigh: Too much potassium from supplements or salt substitutes can be dangerous, especially with kidney disease or certain blood pressure medications.
Food potassium from vegetables, beans, and fruit is rarely a problem for healthy kidneys.

foodSourceGroups.heading: High potassium foods that are neither fruits nor vegetables

male: Higher targets (about 3,400 mg/day) reflect larger body size and typical needs for blood-pressure and workout recovery support.
Useful for men on higher-protein diets who may under-eat produce unless they plan vegetables and legumes.
female: Adequate potassium supports blood pressure and may reduce bloating when sodium is high.
Important across the lifespan; needs stay meaningful even with smaller calorie targets than men.
bowelMovementsAltered: Potassium is lost heavily with chronic diarrhea or vomiting—low levels weaken smooth muscle function in the gut and can prolong irregular bowel patterns.
Bananas, potatoes, beans, and leafy greens replenish potassium alongside fluids when altered bowel movements involve watery or frequent stools.
brainLongevity: Maintains Na⁺/K⁺ ion gradients that power sodium-dependent glutamate transporters (EAAT/GLT) on astrocytes—clearing excess glutamate from the synaptic space.

tooLow: Chronic low calcium can weaken bones over time (osteopenia/osteoporosis), raise fracture risk, and contribute to muscle cramps or tingling in severe cases.
Very low intake plus low vitamin D magnifies bone loss; some people also notice sleep or dental issues when severely deficient.
enough: Enough calcium builds and maintains bone structure, reducing fracture and osteoporosis risk, and supports muscle contraction, blood clotting, and nerve signaling.
Spread intake through the day with vitamin D and weight-bearing activity for best lifelong bone outcomes.
tooHigh: Very high calcium from supplements—especially without enough vitamin K, magnesium, and movement—may contribute to kidney stones or arterial calcification in some people.
Food calcium is generally safer than megadose pills; pair with vitamin D rather than pushing calcium alone.
foodSources: Kale, collard greens, broccoli & bok choy
male: Supports bone density for lifelong strength training and aging; peak bone mass in youth depends on adequate calcium and D.
May modestly support normal testosterone signaling indirectly via overall hormonal health, though D and sleep matter more.
female: Critical before menopause to bank bone mass; after menopause, adequate calcium + D slows bone loss.
Helps reduce risk of stress fractures and supports muscle function during pregnancy and breastfeeding (needs often rise).
coffeeTeaUser: Coffee and tea polyphenols can bind calcium in the gut; caffeine also mildly increases urinary calcium loss—heavy drinkers should mind calcium from food.
adhd: ADHD nutrition stacks lean on several minerals at once—magnesium, zinc, and iron for dopamine signaling. Calcium supports nerve and muscle function, but large calcium doses at the same meal as iron or zinc can compete for absorption.
Stimulant medications often blunt appetite; irregular meals make it easier to run low on the whole mineral bundle, not just one nutrient.
Spread food calcium through the day and keep heavy dairy or calcium supplements away from iron-rich meals when optimizing ferritin for focus.

dashboardTracking: On this dashboard, track iron against its own daily value (% DV)—that is the primary signal.
Iron and copper work together: copper helps mobilize iron for use. But iron from food does not deplete copper the way chronic high-dose zinc can. There is no standard iron-to-copper ratio to aim for.
If copper % DV runs low while iron looks fine, iron may not be usable—check both minerals on their own bars, not as a combined ratio.
tooLow: Low iron depletes ferritin first, then causes fatigue, weakness, pale skin, brittle nails, restless legs, and poor exercise tolerance.
In women, heavy periods, pregnancy, or low red-meat/legume intake are frequent causes; men should investigate unexplained low iron with a clinician.
enough: Adequate iron carries oxygen in hemoglobin, supports energy metabolism, immune function, and cognitive focus—preventing the fatigue and brain fog of creeping deficiency.
Separate coffee/tea/calcium from iron-rich meals by an hour when optimizing absorption.
Mix with Vitamin C if the iron source is non-heme iron.
Heme iron: comes from animal foods like red meat, chicken, fish, and shellfish. It is absorbed more easily and is less affected by other foods.
Non-heme iron: comes from plant foods and fortified foods. It is absorbed less efficiently, but Vitamin C helps a lot; pair it with orange, kiwi, strawberries, lemon, bell pepper, or salsa.
Even though eggs are animal food, much of their iron acts more like non-heme iron, and egg yolk proteins can reduce absorption a bit.
tooHigh: Excess iron—especially from supplements without confirmed deficiency—may raise oxidative stress and is linked with higher cardiovascular risk in some studies.
Men and postmenopausal women should not supplement iron routinely without blood tests.
foodSources: Pair with citrus or peppers for plant sources
male: Lower daily target (about 8 mg) still supports training, thyroid hormones, and dopamine-related energy pathways.
Helps prevent the “always tired despite sleep” pattern when ferritin is borderline low.
female: Higher need (about 18 mg/day premenopause) replaces menstrual losses and supports fertility and pregnancy.
Adequate iron reduces hair shedding, cold intolerance, and brain fog that many women blame on stress alone.
coffeeTeaUser: Coffee, tea, and energy drinks at meals can cut iron absorption substantially—especially tea. Track iron closely and separate caffeine drinks from iron-rich meals by at least an hour.
adhd: Iron is a mandatory cofactor for tyrosine hydroxylase—the rate-limiting enzyme that turns tyrosine into L-DOPA, the immediate precursor to dopamine. Low brain iron stores cap dopamine production regardless of stimulant dose.

Track serum ferritin, not only hemoglobin. Clinical literature often targets ferritin above ~50 ng/mL for neurocognitive function. Low stores show up as severe mental fatigue, restless legs, high impulsivity, and weaker response to stimulant medication.
Food iron plus gentle supplemental forms such as iron bisglycinate (with clinician guidance) are common approaches when ferritin is low.
anemia: Iron is the core of hemoglobin; low iron is the most common anemia cause—fatigue, pallor, and breathlessness.
antiAging: Iron supports cytochrome enzymes in mitochondria that generate ATP and manage oxidative byproducts—low iron starves cellular energy even before anemia appears.
Balance matters: excess iron raises oxidative stress, but adequate food iron with vitamin C supports oxygen delivery and mitochondrial electron transport as you age.
hairLoss: Low ferritin is one of the most common correctable causes of diffuse hair shedding (telogen effluvium)—especially in women with heavy periods, pregnancy, or restrictive diets.
Iron supports oxygen delivery to rapidly dividing hair-follicle cells; pair plant iron with vitamin C and keep coffee/tea away from iron-rich meals when rebuilding stores.

tooLow: Low magnesium shows up as muscle twitches, cramps, poor sleep, anxiety, headaches, and insulin resistance over time.
Chronic stress, alcohol, PPI use, and refined diets low in greens/nuts/seeds increase risk.
enough: Magnesium supports 300+ enzyme reactions: energy (ATP), muscle relaxation, sleep quality, blood sugar control, and blood pressure—helping prevent cramps, migraines, and metabolic drift.
Food sources are first-line; supplements only if diet falls short.
tooHigh: High-dose magnesium supplements can cause diarrhea, low blood pressure, and dangerous heart rhythm changes in extreme cases.
People with kidney disease should avoid unsupervised magnesium supplements.
male: Higher targets (about 420 mg/day) support testosterone production indirectly via sleep, training recovery, and stress resilience.
Often helps gym performance and nighttime leg cramps when diet is magnesium-poor.
female: Useful for PMS cramps, migraine prevention, and sleep in perimenopause; needs (about 320 mg/day) stay high despite smaller body size.
Supports bone health alongside calcium and vitamin D, especially after 40.
coffeeTeaUser: Caffeine increases urinary magnesium loss; heavy coffee, tea, or energy drink use can leave you lower than your food intake suggests.
adhd: Magnesium is a natural antagonist at NMDA (glutamate) receptors—it acts as the nervous system's main molecular brake. Chronic stress and heavy cognitive load increase magnesium loss.

Without enough magnesium, glutamate-driven overexcitation fuels racing thoughts, physical restlessness, and sleep-onset insomnia. Jaw clenching and muscle tension are common, including as a side effect of stimulant medication.

Magnesium glycinate supports systemic relaxation and sleep; magnesium L-threonate crosses the blood–brain barrier more readily for cognitive calm. Magnesium oxide is poorly absorbed and less useful for these goals.
antiAging: Magnesium is required for ATP stability in mitochondria—without it, cells cannot hold or use energy efficiently, which shows up as fatigue and metabolic drift with age.
Low magnesium worsens insulin resistance, blood pressure, and sleep—patterns that accelerate vascular and cognitive aging over decades.
Greens, nuts, seeds, and legumes supply the mitochondrial cofactor most refined diets under-deliver.
bowelMovementsAltered: Low magnesium is a common hidden contributor to constipation; food magnesium (greens, nuts, seeds) and well-tolerated forms like glycinate support softer, more regular stools.
High-dose magnesium supplements (especially oxide or citrate) can loosen stools or cause diarrhea—worth knowing if bowel movements are already too loose.
brainLongevity: Blocks excessive calcium entry through NMDA (glutamate) receptors—complementing astrocyte uptake by preventing excitotoxic overstimulation when synaptic glutamate spikes.

dashboardTracking: On this dashboard, track zinc against its own daily value (% DV)—that is the primary signal.

The main mineral interaction to watch is zinc versus copper: chronic high-dose zinc supplements can lower copper absorption. This app tracks each on its own % DV rather than a zinc-to-copper ratio, which has no standard food-based target.
Food zinc rarely causes copper problems; supplement megadoses are the usual trigger.
tooLow: Low zinc weakens taste/smell, slows wound healing, increases infections, and can cause hair thinning and skin issues.
Plant-heavy diets without legumes/nuts/seeds, or high phytate intake without soaking, raise deficiency risk.
enough: Zinc supports immune defense, wound healing, protein synthesis, and DNA repair—helping you recover from illness and training without frequent infections.
Oysters and varied animal/plant foods usually cover needs without megadoses.
tooHigh: Chronic high-dose zinc supplements can deplete copper, weaken immunity, and cause nausea—more is not better.
Long-term zinc above needs may interfere with copper absorption and HDL cholesterol.
male: Important for sperm quality, libido, and testosterone conversion; athletes lose zinc in sweat.
Shortfalls sometimes show up as poor recovery or frequent colds during heavy training blocks.
female: Supports skin, cycle regularity, and immunity; needs are lower (about 8 mg/day) but still essential in pregnancy.
Helps collagen repair and may reduce acne flares when intake and absorption are adequate.
coffeeTeaUser: Tannins in coffee and tea can bind zinc in the gut alongside iron; chronic caffeine drinkers may need to mind zinc from food.
adhd: Zinc shapes dopamine transporter (DAT) architecture so released dopamine stays in the synapse long enough to bind receptors. Low zinc speeds dopamine clearance and can mimic a functional dopamine shortage.
Low zinc correlates with inattention, weak spatial memory, and needing higher stimulant doses to achieve the same focus. Serum zinc is the usual biomarker to track.
Zinc picolinate or zinc gluconate with food is typical for supplementation; empty-stomach zinc often causes nausea.
hairLoss: Zinc is required for hair-follicle protein synthesis and cell division; deficiency shows up as thinning hair, brittle strands, and slow regrowth.
Plant-heavy diets without legumes, nuts, or seeds—and high phytate intake without soaking—raise zinc shortfall risk alongside iron.
cataractsPrevention: Zinc is a cofactor for lens antioxidant enzymes (including copper/zinc superoxide dismutase) that help neutralize oxidative damage linked with cataract formation.
Adequate zinc from oysters, meat, legumes, and seeds supports eye tissue repair without the copper-depleting risks of chronic high-dose zinc pills.
bowelMovementsAltered: Zinc supports intestinal lining repair and immune balance in the gut; deficiency weakens barrier function and can worsen diarrhea and malabsorption.
Chronic diarrhea depletes zinc, creating a cycle—restoring intake from oysters, meat, legumes, and seeds helps break it when bowel movements stay altered.
brainLongevity: Supports synaptic signaling and modulates glutamate transporter function; low zinc impairs the antioxidant defenses astrocytes rely on during high glutamate load.

tooLow: Clinical vitamin A deficiency is rare in the United States—fewer than 1% of the population shows severe deficiency levels in blood tests—but a significant portion of Americans fall short of meeting optimal daily dietary requirements.

When intake is genuinely low, symptoms include night blindness, dry eyes, poor skin healing, and greater infection risk. Fat malabsorption or very low animal/vegetable intake (preformed A or beta-carotene) are typical causes.

Meeting daily requirements has become harder over time. Studies show that overall dietary intake and crop concentration of vitamin A (and its precursor, beta-carotene) have decreased over the past several decades. This decline comes from two main areas: soil and crop quality reducing nutrient density in produce, and dietary shifts—long-term population studies show a significant drop in average dietary vitamin A intake, largely driven by adults consuming fewer animal products (such as liver and dairy) and less beta-carotene-rich produce (like carrots).
enough: Vitamin A is tracked as total mcg RAE (retinol activity equivalents). Preform retinol and proform β-carotene have different roles—vision/immune gene programs versus antioxidant plus on-demand conversion—and can be logged separately when you know the breakdown.
Beta-carotene from colorful plants converts as needed; preformed A from liver/dairy/eggs is more potent—avoid megadoses of retinol.
tooHigh: Excess preformed vitamin A from supplements or frequent liver can cause liver damage, bone thinning, and birth defects in pregnancy. The IOM adult UL is 3,000 mcg/day of preformed retinol only.
Beta-carotene from plants is safer because the body converts only what it needs; there is no IOM UL for food β-carotene.
male: Supports immune resilience during heavy work or travel and skin recovery after sun or shaving irritation.
Carotenoids act as antioxidants that may help exercise recovery when eaten with fats.
female: Critical for fertility, fetal development, and breastfeeding—too much preformed A in pregnancy is harmful, so food balance matters.
Helps mucous membranes stay healthy, reducing dry-eye and vaginal dryness when overall nutrition is sound.
cataractsPrevention: While vitamins cannot reverse or cure an existing cataract, a balanced diet including vitamin A, vitamin C, vitamin E, and carotenoids (like lutein and zeaxanthin) is linked to a reduced risk of cataract development and progression.
Vitamin A (retinol and carotenoids) supports corneal and retinal health; beta-carotene from colorful produce also feeds the broader carotenoid network that protects eye tissues.
Food sources—sweet potato, leafy greens, eggs—supply vision-supporting A without the cataract-unrelated risks of megadose preformed A supplements.
bowelMovementsAltered: Vitamin A is crucial for a healthy gut barrier. It maintains the physical integrity of the intestinal lining by promoting the expression of tight junction proteins (like ZO-1, claudin-1, and occludin). It also directs mucosal immune cells to the gut and helps beneficial bacteria produce protective retinoic acid.

Metabolized into retinoic acid, vitamin A physically seals the gaps between intestinal cells—strengthening tight junctions and preventing leaky gut (intestinal permeability), which stops harmful pathogens and pro-inflammatory molecules (like LPS) from entering the bloodstream.
Vitamin A prevents gut dysbiosis by supporting the growth of beneficial bacteria and maintaining a diverse microbial community.

It directs critical innate immune cells to the intestines and aids in the formation of regulatory T cells (Tregs), which suppress harmful inflammation in the gut—helpful when bowel habits swing toward diarrhea or cramping from gut inflammation.

dashboardTracking: Log preform vitamin A (retinol / retinyl esters) when you know a food is animal- or fortification-based—liver, eggs, dairy, fortified milk. Omit when only total vitamin A (RAE) is listed.
Scored against the IOM adult UL of 3,000 mcg/day (ceiling): there is no separate FDA Daily Value for retinol alone. Use total vitamin A for % DV toward the RDA.
tooLow: Low preformed retinol matters most when plant carotenoid intake is also low, or conversion is poor (fat malabsorption, very low-fat meals).
Night blindness and dry eyes are classic early signs when total vitamin A activity stays inadequate.
enough: Retinol is the active preform used for vision (rhodopsin), immune and epithelial gene programs, reproduction, and wound healing—distinct from β-carotene’s antioxidant role.
Modest servings of eggs, dairy, or occasional liver usually cover needs when the rest of the diet includes colorful plants.
tooHigh: Chronic intake above the IOM UL (3,000 mcg/day preformed A from food plus supplements) raises risk of liver injury, bone thinning, and birth defects in pregnancy.
Food β-carotene does not count toward this UL; megadose retinol supplements and frequent liver are the usual excess sources.
male: Supports mucosal immunity and skin barrier recovery; keep supplemental retinol modest if liver or fortified foods are already high.
female: Essential in pregnancy and breastfeeding, but excess preformed A is teratogenic—prefer food sources and stay under the UL; β-carotene from plants is the safer high-intake path.
cataractsPrevention: Retinol supports corneal and retinal health; adequate food intake pairs with vitamin C, E, and carotenoids for eye tissue resilience.
Megadose retinol supplements are not a cataract strategy and raise toxicity risk.

dashboardTracking: Log proform vitamin A (β-carotene) as mcg of β-carotene mass (USDA-style), not RAE. Omit when only total vitamin A is listed.
Shown unscored: there is no separate FDA Daily Value, RDA, or recommended retinol∶β-carotene ratio. IOM conversion for totals is 12 mcg dietary β-carotene ≈ 1 mcg RAE (2 mcg if from a supplement).
tooLow: Low β-carotene usually means few orange/green plants—you lose both antioxidant carotenoid intake and a major provitamin A backup when animal retinol is also scarce.
Very low-fat meals reduce carotenoid absorption; pair colorful produce with a little oil or nuts.
enough: β-Carotene is the main dietary proform: it acts as an antioxidant (quenches singlet oxygen) and converts to retinol only as needed—complementary to preformed retinol’s vision and gene-expression roles.
Colorful plants—carrots, sweet potato, leafy greens, mango, cantaloupe—cover most dietary β-carotene when eaten with some fat.
tooHigh: Food β-carotene is not assigned an IOM UL; very high intake can yellow the skin (carotenodermia) without retinol toxicity.
High-dose β-carotene supplements (about 20–30 mg/day) increased lung cancer risk in smokers and asbestos workers (ATBC, CARET)—smokers should avoid those supplements.
male: Plant β-carotene supplies antioxidant cover and provitamin A without the retinol UL ceiling—useful when training diet is produce-heavy with dietary fat.
female: Preferred high-intake path for vitamin A activity in pregnancy because conversion is regulated; still eat with fat for absorption.
cataractsPrevention: β-Carotene feeds the broader carotenoid network that protects eye tissues alongside lutein, zeaxanthin, vitamins C and E.
Food sources are the usual route; high-dose supplements are not advised for smokers.

tooLow: Low vitamin D is linked with fatigue, bone pain, frequent illness, low mood, poor sleep, and muscle weakness.
Limited sun, dark skin at high latitudes, obesity, and aging reduce production; food alone rarely fixes severe deficiency.
enough: Adequate vitamin D helps absorb calcium, regulates immunity, and supports muscle function and mood—lowering fall, fracture, and respiratory infection risk when levels are sufficient.
Some human trials and animal studies show vitamin D supplementation lowers TMAO—possibly by shifting gut microbes (more Bacteroides, less Firmicutes)—especially when you start deficient.
tooHigh: Very high vitamin D from megadose supplements raises blood calcium, which can cause kidney stones, heart rhythm problems, and soft-tissue calcification.
Test levels before taking high-dose vitamin D long term.
male: Associated with healthier testosterone levels, strength, and sperm quality when deficiency is corrected.
Supports sleep depth and daytime energy, which cascade into training consistency and libido.
female: Supports bone density, mood, and immune health; deficiency is common in pregnancy and breastfeeding without supplementation.
May ease cycle-related aches and winter fatigue when levels are brought into range.
adhd: Vitamin D3 acts as a neurosteroid that upregulates neurotrophic factors and helps protect dopaminergic neurons from oxidative stress.
Levels below ~30 ng/mL are associated with worse executive function, working memory, and emotional regulation—symptoms that stack on top of ADHD.
Fatty fish, fortified dairy, safe sun exposure, and tested supplementation are typical ways to correct low status.
antiAging: Vitamin D acts as a senomorphic nutrient—it downregulates chronic age-related inflammation (inflamaging) and supports immune balance rather than only bone health.
Low vitamin D is linked with faster epigenetic aging markers, weaker muscle maintenance, and higher cardiovascular risk over decades.
Adequate D from fatty fish, fortified foods, and safe sun helps cells resist the inflammatory signaling that accelerates tissue decline.
hairLoss: Low vitamin D is linked with telogen effluvium and some forms of alopecia; follicles have vitamin D receptors and need adequate status for normal cycling.
Fatty fish, fortified dairy, safe sun, and tested supplementation are the usual routes when blood levels run low—especially in winter or with limited outdoor time.
bowelMovementsAltered: Vitamin D receptors are abundant in gut tissue; adequate D supports a balanced microbiome (more Bacteroides, less Firmicutes in some studies) and mucosal immunity that keeps inflammation-driven diarrhea or irregularity in check.
Deficiency is linked with IBS-like symptoms and greater gut permeability in some cohorts—fatty fish, fortified dairy, and tested supplementation are typical fixes.

tooLow: B12 deficiency causes fatigue, numbness/tingling, balance problems, memory issues, and megaloblastic anemia—can mimic neuropathy or dementia.

B12 is essential whenever cells divide: each division requires accurate DNA copying. When levels are too low, copying errors can accumulate—over many years raising the risk of certain cancers, particularly colon cancer.
Vegans, older adults with low stomach acid, and people on metformin or long-term PPIs are high-risk groups.
enough: B12 is required for red blood cells, nerve myelination, DNA synthesis, and homocysteine control.

In a one-year trial, vitamin D plus B12, B6, and folate lowered fasting TMAO more than vitamin D alone—likely through homocysteine and choline metabolism cross-talk, though mechanisms are still unclear.
tooHigh: While deficiency can harm DNA, some studies link very high B12—especially from long-term high-dose supplements—to lung, pancreatic, and liver cancer, certain myeloid blood cancers, and poorer outcomes in some cancer patients.
Food sources are safe at normal intake; routine megadose supplements beyond correcting deficiency are rarely warranted.
male: Supports energy, focus, and exercise performance via oxygen delivery and nerve speed.
Helps keep homocysteine in check, which matters for cardiovascular risk alongside folate.
female: Critical before and during pregnancy to prevent neural tube and developmental issues (with folate).
Low B12 can drive anemia that feels like “burnout” and hair loss, often overlooked in busy women.
adhd: B12 partners with folate and B6 in one-carbon metabolism and methylation; without adequate B12, folate-driven dopamine support is incomplete.
Low B12 mimics brain fog, slow processing, and fatigue that are easy to attribute to ADHD alone.
Vegans, older adults, and people on metformin or long-term acid suppressants are higher-risk groups.
anemia: B12 deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia—large, ineffective red cells; needs testing if iron is normal but you are tired.
antiAging: B12 protects DNA integrity during cell division and supports mitochondrial methyl-group chemistry with folate.
Low B12 raises homocysteine and mimics accelerated cognitive aging—correcting deficiency is one of the clearest nutrition wins for older adults.
hairLoss: B12 deficiency can trigger megaloblastic anemia and diffuse hair shedding that feels like stress-related loss—test B12 when iron is normal but shedding persists.
Vegans, older adults, and people on metformin or long-term acid suppressants are higher-risk groups for B12-driven fatigue and hair changes.
bowelMovementsAltered: B12 deficiency from low stomach acid, metformin, or vegan diets can present with diarrhea or constipation alongside fatigue and neuropathy—gut motility changes are an underrecognized clue.
Correcting B12 when deficient often stabilizes digestion that had drifted toward loose or sluggish bowel habits.

tooLow: Smokers need more; very low fruit/vegetable intake or heat/light-degraded foods increase risk.
enough: Vitamin C builds collagen for skin, joints, and blood vessels; supports immunity; improves plant-iron absorption; and acts as an antioxidant against pollution and exercise stress.
Regular produce intake easily covers daily needs for most people and helps prevent scurvy-related bleeding gums and slow healing.
tooHigh: Megadose vitamin C supplements may cause digestive upset and, in predisposed people, increase kidney stone risk.
Food sources are safe at normal intake; very high pills are unnecessary for most people.
male: Higher target (about 90 mg/day) supports collagen for joints/tendons under heavy training loads.
Antioxidant role may reduce oxidative stress from intense exercise and pollution exposure.
female: Supports skin elasticity, iron absorption during higher iron needs, and immune defense.
Useful for collagen during pregnancy and for healing after procedures or injury.
coffeeTeaUser: Vitamin C helps recover iron absorption when you eat iron-rich foods—even if you drink coffee or tea at other meals.
adhd: Vitamin C is a required cofactor for dopamine β-hydroxylase—the enzyme that converts dopamine into norepinephrine. Low vitamin C can bottleneck that step even when other dopamine pathways are supported.
It also strongly improves absorption of plant-based iron, which matters because adequate brain iron (ferritin) is needed for tyrosine hydroxylase and dopamine production in ADHD.
Regular produce at iron-containing meals supports both the iron–dopamine axis and catecholamine balance; deficiency contributes to fatigue and fog that overlap with ADHD symptoms.
anemia: Vitamin C improves plant-iron absorption and supports healthy blood vessels when rebuilding iron stores.
antiAging: Vitamin C neutralizes free radicals in blood and tissues, recycling vitamin E and protecting DNA from oxidative hits that trigger cellular aging.
It supports collagen repair and immune surveillance—both decline with age when antioxidant intake is low.
Regular produce intake is the practical shield against oxidative stress; megadose pills are not required for this longevity role.
hairLoss: Vitamin C builds collagen for the scalp and hair shaft and strongly improves plant-iron absorption—both matter when iron-driven shedding is part of the picture.
Regular produce at iron-containing meals helps rebuild ferritin while supporting connective tissue around follicles.
cataractsPrevention: The lens concentrates vitamin C as an antioxidant buffer against UV and oxidative damage—one reason produce-rich diets correlate with lower cataract risk in population studies.
Vitamin C also recycles vitamin E in eye tissues; citrus, peppers, and leafy greens supply the water-soluble half of that antioxidant pair.
brainLongevity: Astrocytes concentrate vitamin C as a primary brain antioxidant, recycling glutathione and protecting against oxidative stress from glutamate turnover.

tooLow: People who avoid iodized salt, seafood, and dairy are at higher risk, especially during pregnancy.
enough: Iodine is essential for thyroid hormones that regulate metabolism, body temperature, and brain development—preventing goiter, fatigue, and cognitive sluggishness when intake is steady.

Iodized salt is standard table salt with trace iodine added—usually as potassium iodide. Salt iodization is a simple, highly effective public-health measure designed to prevent deficiency and support healthy thyroid function. About ¼ teaspoon of iodized salt provides roughly 52% of the daily value.
Seafood, dairy, and eggs are other dependable sources; most adults need only small amounts day to day.
tooHigh: For most adults, the recommended daily dietary allowance is 150 μg, with a safe upper limit of 1,100 μg per day. Overdosing is rare, but can happen if you regularly eat excessive amounts of seaweed/kelp or take too many supplements.
Excess iodine from high-dose supplements or daily seaweed megadoses can trigger or worsen thyroid dysfunction—especially in people with autoimmune thyroid disease.
Iodized salt in normal cooking amounts is rarely a problem; watch concentrated supplements.
foodSources: Iodized table salt (Morton Iodized Salt, 26 oz—widely in stores)
male: Adequate iodine supports thyroid-driven energy, temperature regulation, and workout recovery.
Very high intake from supplements or seaweed megadoses can also disrupt thyroid function.
female: Iodine needs rise in pregnancy and breastfeeding for fetal brain development.
Low iodine can worsen cycle-related fatigue and hair thinning when thyroid function dips.

tooLow: Low B6 can cause anemia-like fatigue, cracked lips, depression, and weakened immunity; severe deficiency damages nerves.
Heavy alcohol use, some medications, and very processed diets increase risk.
enough: Vitamin B6 supports neurotransmitters, homocysteine metabolism, and immune function—working with folate and B12 in one-carbon metabolism.
It was included in the B-vitamin arm that lowered TMAO alongside vitamin D in a year-long trial; correcting low B6 may help cardiovascular and gut-metabolite balance.
tooHigh: Very high B6 supplements (often 50–100 mg) for months can cause nerve tingling and numbness—stay near food-level intake unless prescribed.
Chronic megadoses are not needed for TMAO and can harm nerves.
male: Supports testosterone-related enzyme pathways indirectly and helps process homocysteine when eating high-protein diets.
Athletes on heavy training may need consistent intake for energy metabolism and mood.
female: Useful for PMS mood symptoms and nausea in pregnancy (often combined with ginger); works with folate for homocysteine control.
Oral contraceptives can increase B6 needs slightly in some women.
adhd: Pyridoxal-5-phosphate (P5P) is the active form of vitamin B6 that drives conversion of L-DOPA into dopamine—it completes the catecholamine chain after iron-dependent tyrosine hydroxylase.
Standard pyridoxine HCl must be converted in the liver before the brain can use it; P5P is immediately available for neurotransmitter enzymes.
Low B6 worsens attention swings and mood instability when the dopamine synthesis pipeline is already under strain.
anemia: B6 deficiency can cause sideroblastic anemia and nerve symptoms; often overlooked when only iron is checked.
antiAging: B6 supports mitochondrial enzyme function and homocysteine control—high homocysteine damages blood vessels and is linked with faster cognitive aging.
Together with folate and B12, B6 keeps one-carbon metabolism running so cells can repair DNA and manage oxidative stress.
brainLongevity: Needed for glutamate decarboxylase, which converts glutamate into GABA—helping keep excitation–inhibition balance alongside astrocyte clearance.

tooLow: Heavy alcohol use, certain medications, and diets without greens/legumes are common causes.
enough: Folate (B9) supports DNA synthesis, red blood cell formation, and methylation reactions that affect mood and homocysteine—a marker tied to cardiovascular risk.
Together with B12 and B6, folate was part of the combination that lowered TMAO alongside vitamin D in clinical research—adequacy matters more than megadoses.
tooHigh: Very high folic acid from supplements can mask B12 deficiency, allowing nerve damage to progress unnoticed.
Food folate is generally safe; separate high-dose folic acid from unchecked B12 status.
male: Helps cardiovascular health via homocysteine control and supports energy through healthy red blood cell production.
Adequate folate supports sperm DNA integrity, which matters for fertility.
female: Essential before conception and in early pregnancy for neural tube development (often supplemented as folic acid).
Supports energy and mood when paired with B12; needs stay at 400 mcg DFE for many adult women not pregnant.
adhd: Folate (B9) fuels the methylation cycle that produces S-adenosylmethionine (SAMe). SAMe is directly used to synthesize dopamine, serotonin, and norepinephrine in the brain.

Synthetic folic acid must be converted through several steps before it can cross the blood–brain barrier. Up to ~40% of people carry MTHFR variants (e.g. C677T, A1298C) that slow this conversion—depriving the brain of active folate even when folic acid intake looks adequate on paper.

Adults with poor folate activation often show treatment-resistant ADHD symptoms, persistent brain fog, and overlapping mood issues. Active forms such as L-methylfolate (L-5-MTHF) or folinic acid bypass MTHFR bottlenecks and feed neurotransmitter pathways more directly.
anemia: Folate deficiency also causes megaloblastic anemia; important to distinguish from B12 deficiency clinically.
antiAging: Folate drives DNA repair and methylation—processes that slow when cells accumulate damage during aging.
Adequate folate with B12 and B6 supports homocysteine clearance and protects vascular and brain tissue from oxidative injury over decades.
hairLoss: Folate supports rapid cell division in the hair bulb; low folate alongside B12 can cause anemia-like shedding and brittle regrowth.
Active folate forms (L-methylfolate) matter when MTHFR variants slow conversion—folate status is worth checking when B12 and iron look normal.
bowelMovementsAltered: Folate deficiency alongside B12 can cause megaloblastic changes that affect the GI tract—mouth soreness, diarrhea, or poor absorption feeding back on bowels.
Celiac disease, IBD, and chronic diarrhea impair folate absorption; leafy greens, legumes, and fortified grains help when gut inflammation drives altered stool patterns.

tooLow: Long-term raw egg whites (avidin blocks absorption), some antibiotics, and rare metabolic disorders increase deficiency risk.
enough: Biotin (B7) supports keratin production for hair and nails, fatty acid synthesis, and glucose metabolism—helping energy and skin barrier health.
Most people cover needs from eggs, nuts, and varied whole foods without supplements.
tooHigh: Biotin from food is safe; very high supplement doses can interfere with some lab tests (especially thyroid and troponin)—tell clinicians before blood work.
Megadoses are not needed for hair or nails unless a clinician confirms deficiency.
male: Supports healthy hair and nail turnover and helps process fats and carbs during heavy training blocks.
Adequate intake supports skin recovery and energy metabolism without megadose supplements.
female: Often marketed for hair and nails; food sources usually suffice unless deficiency is confirmed.
Needs stay at about 30 mcg/day through pregnancy and breastfeeding—eggs, nuts, and produce cover most diets.
antiAging: Biotin supports enzymes that build fatty acids and metabolize carbs—both feed mitochondrial energy production.
Low biotin impairs glucose handling and skin barrier repair, subtle deficits that compound metabolic aging over time.
hairLoss: Biotin (B7) supports keratin production—the structural protein in hair and nails; deficiency can cause thinning, though true biotin deficiency is uncommon outside raw-egg-white diets or long antibiotic courses.
Eggs, nuts, salmon, and varied whole foods usually cover needs; megadose biotin supplements can skew lab tests and are rarely needed without confirmed deficiency.

tooLow: Low thiamin (B1) causes fatigue, irritability, poor appetite, and nerve problems; severe deficiency leads to beriberi.
Without enough thiamin, your body cannot efficiently convert carbohydrates into usable energy—instead, carbs are diverted into lactic acid, which can build up to dangerous levels (lactic acidosis).
Heavy alcohol use, very refined diets, and frequent vomiting raise deficiency risk.
enough: Thiamin helps convert carbs into energy and supports nerve and heart muscle function—keeping you alert and steady during long workdays.
Most varied diets with grains, pork, or legumes cover needs without supplements.
tooHigh: Thiamin from food is safe; high-dose supplements are rarely needed and can cause stomach upset in sensitive people.
Water-soluble excess is usually excreted—megadoses do not boost energy beyond correcting deficiency.
male: Supports energy metabolism during heavy training and long shifts when carb intake is high.
Alcohol-heavy weeks deplete thiamin faster—whole grains and legumes help restore balance.
female: Important during pregnancy and breastfeeding when energy and nerve demands rise.
Helps mood and focus when paired with other B vitamins in a balanced diet.
antiAging: Thiamin (B1) is required for pyruvate dehydrogenase—the step that feeds carbohydrate breakdown into the mitochondrial Krebs cycle.
Low thiamin traps cells in low-energy metabolism and raises lactic acid, a pattern that accelerates fatigue and vascular stress with age.
bowelMovementsAltered: Severe thiamin (B1) deficiency causes beriberi, which disrupts autonomic nerve control of the digestive tract—leading to constipation, loss of appetite, indigestion, and in wet beriberi, abdominal distension.
Adequate thiamin keeps the nerve signals that drive normal gut motility and appetite functioning; beriberi-class deficiency is a classic cause of altered bowel habits alongside fatigue and weakness.
brainLongevity: Feeds pyruvate into the Krebs cycle for neuronal and astrocyte ATP—glutamate uptake and conversion to glutamine is energy-intensive.

tooLow: Low riboflavin (B2) can cause cracked lips, sore throat, skin inflammation, and anemia-like fatigue.
Vegan diets without dairy or fortified foods and heavy alcohol use increase risk.
enough: Riboflavin supports energy production, antioxidant enzymes, and healthy skin and eyes—helping you recover from daily oxidative stress.
Milk, eggs, and leafy greens usually cover needs in mixed diets.
tooHigh: Riboflavin from food is safe; excess turns urine bright yellow harmlessly.
Supplements above needs rarely help performance and are unnecessary for most people.
male: Supports antioxidant defenses during sun exposure and heavy exercise.
Helps process fats and proteins when eating high-protein diets.
female: Supports skin and eye health; needs stay steady through pregnancy at about 1.4 mg/day.
Oral contraceptives may slightly increase riboflavin turnover in some women.
anemia: Low B2 can cause anemia-like fatigue and cracked lips; part of the B-vitamin cluster that builds red blood cells.
antiAging: Riboflavin (B2) builds FAD—another mitochondrial electron carrier paired with NAD in the energy-production chain.
Low riboflavin weakens antioxidant enzyme systems (glutathione reductase) that protect cells from the oxidative stress that drives aging.
brainLongevity: Builds FAD for glutathione reductase and MTHFR-linked methylation—supporting the antioxidant recycling astrocytes depend on during glutamate cleanup.

tooLow: Very low-protein or corn-heavy diets without variety are rare causes in developed countries.
enough: Niacin supports energy metabolism, DNA repair, and cholesterol balance at food-level intakes—keeping cells fueled and skin healthy.
Poultry, fish, peanuts, and fortified grains typically cover needs.
tooHigh: High-dose niacin supplements (often 500 mg+) can cause flushing, liver stress, and glucose changes—prescription doses need monitoring.
Food niacin is safe; megadoses are not needed for heart health without medical supervision.
male: Higher calorie intake generally means higher niacin needs—protein-rich meals cover most active men.
Supports energy turnover during heavy training blocks.
female: Needs are slightly lower (about 14 mg NE/day) but still essential for skin and energy.
Adequate niacin supports mood and metabolism alongside other B vitamins.
antiAging: Niacin (vitamin B3) is the direct food precursor for NAD+—the coenzyme mitochondria use to transfer electrons and rebuild cellular energy capacity.
NAD+ pools decline with age; adequate niacin from poultry, fish, peanuts, and whole grains supports the NAD salvage pathway that keeps mitochondria fueled.
Food niacin supports longevity through energy metabolism—not the high-dose niacin supplement protocols studied for lipids.
brainLongevity: Main food precursor for NAD+, which fuels mitochondrial ATP production in astrocytes during energy-intensive glutamate clearance.

tooLow: Low pantothenic acid (B5) is rare but can cause fatigue, numbness, and burning feet in severe deficiency.
Only extreme malnutrition or rare metabolic disorders typically cause noticeable shortfalls.
enough: Pantothenic acid helps make coenzyme A for energy, hormones, and fat metabolism—supporting steady energy and stress resilience.
It is widespread in animal and plant foods, so varied diets usually cover the ~5 mg/day target.
tooHigh: Pantothenic acid from food is safe; very high supplements may cause diarrhea in some people.
Megadoses are not proven to boost energy or hair growth beyond correcting deficiency.
male: Supports hormone and energy metabolism during high-stress or heavy training periods.
Found in most protein-rich meals—shortfalls are uncommon with varied eating.
female: Needs stay at about 5 mg/day through pregnancy and breastfeeding.
Works with other B vitamins for energy and adrenal-related stress pathways.
antiAging: Pantothenic acid (B5) builds coenzyme A—the molecule that links fat and carb breakdown to mitochondrial ATP production.
Without adequate B5, cells cannot efficiently funnel nutrients into the Krebs cycle, which shows up as low energy and poor stress tolerance over time.

tooLow: Low chromium is hard to diagnose; some research links inadequate intake to poorer blood sugar control, but clear deficiency symptoms are uncommon.
Very refined diets low in whole grains and vegetables may provide less chromium.
enough: Chromium may help insulin sensitivity and glucose metabolism at adequate intakes—supporting steady energy between meals.
Broccoli, whole grains, and varied whole foods usually supply enough without supplements.
tooHigh: Chromium supplements in high doses have unclear long-term safety; stick to food sources unless a clinician recommends otherwise.
Industrial hexavalent chromium is toxic—that is not the form in food or typical supplements.
male: May support glucose handling during heavy training and high-carb meals—whole grains and broccoli are practical sources.
Adequate intake fits into a longevity-minded diet without relying on pills.
female: Some studies link adequate chromium to better insulin sensitivity; needs are slightly lower (about 25 mcg/day).
Whole-food sources are preferable to high-dose chromium picolinate supplements.

tooLow: Low selenium weakens thyroid hormone conversion (T4→T3), impairs immune defense, and may increase oxidative damage to cells over time.
Severe deficiency is rare in developed countries but can occur with very restricted diets, certain GI conditions, or in regions with selenium-poor soil.
enough: Selenium supports thyroid function, immune defense, and antioxidant enzymes (glutathione peroxidase and thioredoxin reductase) that protect cells from oxidative damage.
Just 1–2 Brazil nuts daily can meet selenium needs; seafood, meat, and eggs are reliable sources.
tooHigh: Chronic excess selenium (selenosis) causes garlic breath, brittle nails, hair loss, and nerve damage—mostly from high-dose supplements, not food.
Upper limit is 400 mcg/day; avoid stacking multiple selenium-containing supplements.
male: Supports sperm motility and thyroid-driven energy metabolism; athletes may benefit from adequate selenium for recovery.
Adequate selenium with vitamin E supports antioxidant defense during heavy training.
female: Important for thyroid health, which affects cycle regularity, energy, and metabolism.
Adequate selenium supports immune function during pregnancy and may reduce preeclampsia risk.
antiAging: Selenium powers glutathione peroxidase—one of the body's primary antioxidant enzyme systems that neutralizes peroxides before they damage DNA and mitochondrial membranes.
Low selenium accelerates oxidative aging; adequate intake from Brazil nuts, seafood, and eggs supports cellular defense without the risks of megadose supplements.
cataractsPrevention: Selenium supports glutathione peroxidase in lens tissue—an enzyme that neutralizes hydrogen peroxide and lipid peroxides linked with cataract formation.
Brazil nuts, seafood, and eggs supply selenium without the risks of high-dose supplements; works alongside vitamins C and E for eye antioxidant defense.
brainLongevity: Powers glutathione peroxidase in brain tissue, neutralizing peroxides before they damage astrocyte and neuronal membranes during glutamate metabolism.

dashboardTracking: On this dashboard, track copper against its own daily value (% DV)—that is the primary signal.
Copper supports iron metabolism; low copper can look like iron deficiency even when iron intake is adequate. Watch both nutrients on their own % DV bars, not as a combined ratio.
Chronic high-dose zinc is what most often depletes copper—not iron from food.
tooLow: Low copper impairs iron metabolism (causing anemia despite adequate iron), weakens connective tissue, and may cause neurological symptoms.
Very low intake, excess zinc supplements, or malabsorption can deplete copper stores.
enough: Copper supports iron absorption, collagen/elastin formation, energy production in mitochondria, and immune function.
It is also a cofactor for superoxide dismutase (SOD)—a key antioxidant enzyme—and for dopamine β-hydroxylase in neurotransmitter synthesis.
tooHigh: Excess copper from supplements or contaminated water can cause liver damage, nausea, and oxidative stress.
Wilson's disease is a genetic condition of copper overload; most people with varied diets don't need copper supplements.
male: Supports connective tissue and joint health during heavy training; works with zinc for immune defense.
High-dose zinc supplements without copper can deplete copper—balance matters.
female: Supports collagen for skin and bone health; important alongside iron for preventing anemia.
Oral contraceptives can raise serum copper; most women cover needs from a varied diet.
antiAging: Copper is required for cytochrome c oxidase (Complex IV in mitochondria) and for copper/zinc SOD—both protect against the oxidative damage that drives cellular aging.
Balance with zinc is key; neither too much nor too little supports longevity.

tooLow: Low manganese is rare but can impair bone formation, glucose metabolism, and antioxidant defense (manganese SOD).
Very refined diets without whole grains, nuts, or tea may provide insufficient manganese.
enough: Manganese supports bone formation, blood clotting, and mitochondrial antioxidant defense via manganese superoxide dismutase (MnSOD).
Also involved in carbohydrate and amino acid metabolism; whole grains, nuts, and tea are top sources.
tooHigh: Excess manganese from supplements or contaminated water can be neurotoxic—causing tremors and cognitive issues resembling Parkinson's.
Food manganese is safe; supplementation beyond food levels is rarely warranted.
male: Supports bone density and connective tissue integrity during physical training.
Whole grains and nuts in a varied diet typically cover the 2.3 mg/day target.
female: Important for bone health alongside calcium and vitamin D; needs are about 1.8 mg/day.
Tea drinkers often get adequate manganese without trying.
antiAging: Manganese SOD (MnSOD) is the primary antioxidant enzyme inside mitochondria—it neutralizes superoxide radicals right where they're produced during energy generation.
Adequate manganese from whole grains, nuts, and legumes supports this frontline mitochondrial defense against aging.

tooLow: Low vitamin E weakens cell membrane protection against oxidative damage—showing up as muscle weakness, vision issues, and impaired immunity over time.
Fat malabsorption conditions, very low-fat diets, and genetic disorders can cause deficiency.
enough: Vitamin E (primarily alpha-tocopherol) protects cell membranes from lipid peroxidation, supports immune function, and works with vitamin C to regenerate antioxidant capacity.
Nuts, seeds, and plant oils are the richest sources; adequate fat intake helps absorption.
tooHigh: High-dose vitamin E supplements (above 400 IU) may increase bleeding risk and have shown mixed results in trials—food sources are preferred.
Excess can interfere with vitamin K-dependent blood clotting.
male: Supports recovery from oxidative stress during intense exercise; protects PUFA-rich cell membranes in muscle tissue.
Works with selenium for antioxidant enzyme systems.
female: Supports skin health, immune defense, and may ease menstrual discomfort in some women.
Important during pregnancy for fetal development; food sources are safer than high-dose supplements.
antiAging: Vitamin E is the primary fat-soluble antioxidant protecting cell membranes from the lipid peroxidation chain reaction that damages mitochondria, neurons, and cardiovascular tissue.
Works in tandem with vitamin C (which regenerates oxidized vitamin E) and selenium (which powers glutathione enzymes)—together they form the core antioxidant triad against aging.
hairLoss: Vitamin E protects scalp lipids from oxidative damage and supports blood flow to hair follicles; deficiency can weaken the lipid environment hair needs to grow.
Nuts, seeds, and avocado supply vitamin E alongside healthy fats that support follicle health.
cataractsPrevention: Vitamin E protects lens cell membranes from lipid peroxidation—a key mechanism in age-related cataract formation.
Works with vitamin C (which regenerates spent vitamin E) to maintain antioxidant defense in eye tissues; nuts, seeds, and avocado are practical food sources.

dashboardTracking: Track total vitamin K when food labels or databases do not specify K1 vs K2—this row is scored against the FDA daily value.
When you know the form split, also log vitamin K1 and/or vitamin K2 separately; those rows are shown unscored because there is no separate FDA DV for each form.
tooLow: Low vitamin K impairs blood clotting (easy bruising, excessive bleeding) and weakens bone mineralization over time.
Newborns are routinely given vitamin K at birth; adults on blood thinners or with fat malabsorption may be at risk.
enough: Vitamin K activates clotting factors and osteocalcin (for bone mineralization), and K2 specifically directs calcium into bones and away from arteries.
K1 comes from leafy greens; K2 from fermented foods, egg yolks, and animal products—both forms matter for longevity.
tooHigh: Food vitamin K is safe; very high supplemental K2 may interact with blood thinners (warfarin)—consult a clinician if on anticoagulants.
Toxicity from food is essentially unknown.
male: Supports bone density and may reduce arterial calcification risk—especially with adequate vitamin D and calcium.
K2 (MK-7 from natto, MK-4 from animal foods) has the longest-studied cardiovascular benefits.
female: Critical for bone health alongside calcium and vitamin D—especially important post-menopause when bone loss accelerates.
Adequate K2 helps direct calcium to bones rather than soft tissue.
antiAging: Vitamin K2 activates matrix Gla protein (MGP) which prevents calcium from depositing in arteries—arterial calcification is a hallmark of vascular aging.
K1 supports clotting; K2 supports the calcium-routing system that keeps bones strong and arteries flexible with age.

dashboardTracking: Log vitamin K1 (phylloquinone) when you know a food is plant-based K—leafy greens, broccoli, and plant oils. Omit when only total vitamin K is listed.
Shown unscored: there is no separate FDA daily value for K1. Use total vitamin K for % DV when the breakdown is unknown.
tooLow: Low K1 (phylloquinone) mainly affects clotting and bone proteins when green vegetable intake is very low.
Consistent leafy greens usually cover K1.
enough: K1 from plants supports clotting and works with vitamin D and calcium for bone metabolism.
A serving of leafy greens most days is a simple baseline.
tooHigh: Food K1 is safe; high supplemental K1 may interact with blood thinners (warfarin)—consult a clinician if on anticoagulants.
male: K1 activates clotting factors and osteocalcin for bone mineralization alongside vitamin D and calcium.
female: K1 supports bone matrix proteins—especially important when green vegetable intake is inconsistent.
antiAging: K1 supports the clotting and bone-protein systems that work with vitamin D and calcium as you age.

dashboardTracking: Log vitamin K2 (menaquinone) when you know a food supplies animal or fermented K2—natto, cheese, egg yolks, liver. Omit when only total vitamin K is listed.
Shown unscored: there is no separate FDA daily value for K2. MK-4 and MK-7 are tracked separately when you know the menaquinone type.
tooLow: Low K2 (menaquinone) is debated for bone and arterial health, especially with high calcium and low K overall.
Fermented foods and animal products supply most dietary K2.
enough: Vitamin K2 (menaquinone) is found in fermented foods and animal products. It helps route calcium into bone and teeth and may reduce calcium buildup in arteries—not the same job as vitamin K1, which mainly supports blood clotting.
Natto, aged cheese, and egg yolks are notable sources. MK-4 clears within hours (spread through the day); MK-7 lasts for days (once-daily is usually enough).
tooHigh: Food K2 is safe; very high supplemental K2 may interact with blood thinners (warfarin)—consult a clinician if on anticoagulants.
male: K2 (MK-7 from natto, MK-4 from animal foods) may reduce arterial calcification risk when intake is adequate.
female: K2 helps direct calcium to bones rather than soft tissue—especially relevant post-menopause.
antiAging: K2 activates matrix Gla protein (MGP) which prevents calcium from depositing in arteries—a hallmark of vascular aging.

tooLow: Low MK-4 (menaquinone-4) from animal foods may leave gaps if you rely on a single daily meal for K2—its half-life in the body is only a few hours.
Egg yolks, meat, and dairy supply most dietary MK-4; very low intake is common on plant-forward diets without supplementation.
enough: MK-4 is found naturally in some meats and dairy and activates K-dependent proteins for bone and calcium routing.
Because MK-4 clears quickly, spreading intake across meals—or multiple smaller doses if supplementing—helps keep levels steadier through the day.
tooHigh: Food MK-4 is safe; high supplemental MK-4 may interact with blood thinners (warfarin)—consult a clinician if on anticoagulants.
male: MK-4 from animal foods supports the same calcium-routing proteins as other K2 forms—timing matters more than total weekly average.
female: MK-4 helps direct calcium to bone; post-menopause, steady K2 intake across the day may matter more when ovarian estrogen is low.
antiAging: MK-4 activates matrix Gla protein (MGP) and osteocalcin, helping keep calcium in bone rather than soft tissues as you age.
Short half-life means day-to-day consistency beats one large weekly dose for maintaining active K2-dependent proteins.

tooLow: Low MK-7 (menaquinone-7) is common without fermented foods or targeted K2 supplements—many natto products have K2 removed.
Without MK-7 or other long-chain menaquinones, you may miss the form best studied for steady once-daily supplementation.
enough: MK-7 from fermented foods like natto has a much longer half-life (days), allowing steady once-daily intake from food or supplements.
When supplementing K2, choose the MK-7 form rather than generic K2 labels that may not specify menaquinone type.
tooHigh: Food MK-7 is safe; very high supplemental MK-7 may interact with blood thinners (warfarin)—consult a clinician if on anticoagulants.
foodSources: Natto (fermented soy — check label; some products remove K2)
MK-7 supplements (when food intake is low)
male: MK-7's longer half-life makes it practical for once-daily coverage of K2-dependent calcium routing between animal-food meals.
female: MK-7 supports bone and arterial calcium handling—especially useful when fermented foods are not a regular part of the diet.
antiAging: MK-7 sustains matrix Gla protein activation over days rather than hours, supporting arterial flexibility and bone mineralization with less frequent dosing.
Natto is the richest food source, but many commercial natto supplements strip K2—verify labels or use an MK-7-specific supplement.

tooLow: Low phosphorus (hypophosphatemia) causes muscle weakness, bone pain, and impaired energy metabolism—rare from diet alone but possible with antacid overuse or refeeding syndrome.
Alcoholism and certain kidney conditions can deplete phosphorus.
enough: Phosphorus is the second most abundant mineral in the body—essential for bone structure (as hydroxyapatite with calcium), ATP energy currency, DNA/RNA backbone, and cell membrane phospholipids.
Most diets easily meet needs from dairy, meat, legumes, and grains.
tooHigh: Excess phosphorus (often from processed food additives) can pull calcium from bones and promote vascular calcification—especially concerning with impaired kidney function.
High phosphorus with low calcium intake accelerates bone loss over time.
male: Supports bone density and ATP production for energy during training.
Most men easily meet the 700 mg/day target from protein-rich diets.
female: Important for bone structure alongside calcium; needs stay at 700 mg/day.
Excessive phosphorus from processed foods without adequate calcium can undermine bone health.
antiAging: Phosphorus is required for every ATP molecule the body produces—without it, cells cannot store or transfer energy.
Balance matters: excess phosphorus (from food additives) accelerates vascular calcification and may shorten lifespan via FGF23 signaling.

tooLow: Low choline impairs liver function (fatty liver), brain development, and cell membrane integrity—it's essential but often under-consumed.
Muscle damage, memory issues, and elevated homocysteine can signal inadequate choline intake.
enough: Choline builds cell membranes (phosphatidylcholine), supports neurotransmitter synthesis (acetylcholine for memory and muscle control), and provides methyl groups for DNA regulation.
Eggs are the richest common source; liver, fish, and cruciferous vegetables also contribute.
tooHigh: Very high choline intake (above 3500 mg/day) can cause fishy body odor, sweating, low blood pressure, and liver toxicity.
Gut bacteria convert excess choline to TMAO—a metabolite linked to cardiovascular risk at high levels.
male: Higher target (550 mg/day) supports muscle function and liver health; athletes may benefit from adequate choline for endurance.
Works with folate and B12 in methylation; shortfall is common without eggs or liver.
female: Critical during pregnancy for fetal brain development; needs rise to 450 mg/day pregnant, 550 mg lactating.
Many women fall short of the 425 mg/day target—eggs and cruciferous vegetables help close the gap.
antiAging: Choline supports methylation alongside folate and B12—the epigenetic maintenance system that keeps gene expression patterns stable as cells age.
Adequate choline protects against fatty liver and supports acetylcholine production for cognitive function into older age.

tooLow: Molybdenum deficiency is extremely rare from diet—only documented in people on long-term parenteral nutrition without supplementation.
Theoretically, low molybdenum would impair sulfite detoxification and purine metabolism.
enough: Molybdenum is a cofactor for enzymes that process sulfites, purines, and certain drugs—supporting detoxification and uric acid production.
Legumes, grains, and nuts easily cover the 45 mcg/day target in most diets.
tooHigh: Very high molybdenum (above 2 mg/day from supplements) may interfere with copper absorption and raise uric acid.
Food sources are safe; supplementation is rarely needed.
male: Supports sulfite oxidase and xanthine oxidase enzymes; easily met from a varied diet with legumes and grains.
No special considerations beyond general adequacy.
female: Same 45 mcg/day target as men; deficiency is essentially unheard of with normal eating patterns.
Legumes, whole grains, and dairy cover needs without supplementation.

tooLow: Low chloride is rare from diet but can occur with prolonged vomiting, diarrhea, or certain diuretics—causing metabolic alkalosis.
Symptoms include muscle cramps, weakness, and irregular heartbeat.
enough: Chloride maintains fluid balance, supports stomach acid production (HCl for digestion), and helps nerve signal transmission.
Nearly always consumed alongside sodium (as table salt, NaCl); tracking sodium effectively tracks chloride.
tooHigh: Excess chloride follows excess sodium—the same blood pressure concerns apply.
Reducing processed food and added salt addresses both sodium and chloride excess.
male: Adequate intake about 2300 mg/day; effectively matched with sodium recommendations.
Athletes losing heavy sweat may need more—replaced via normal salted foods or electrolyte drinks.
female: Same 2300 mg/day adequate intake; concerns mirror sodium guidance.
Rarely a standalone concern—address through overall sodium balance.

enough: Histidine is an essential amino acid that produces histamine (immune signaling), carnosine (muscle buffer), and supports hemoglobin structure.
Found in meat, fish, dairy, and legumes; deficiency is rare with adequate protein intake.
Isoleucine is a branched-chain amino acid (BCAA) essential for muscle repair, energy regulation during exercise, and immune function.
Works with leucine and valine for muscle protein synthesis; found in eggs, meat, dairy, and soy.
Leucine is the most potent BCAA trigger for muscle protein synthesis (mTOR activation)—critical for building and maintaining muscle mass.
Higher leucine meals (2.5–3 g per serving) maximize the muscle-building signal; important for athletes and older adults preventing sarcopenia.
Lysine supports collagen synthesis, calcium absorption, immune function, and carnitine production for fat metabolism.
Often the limiting amino acid in grain-based diets; legumes and animal foods are rich sources.
Methionine is an essential sulfur-containing amino acid that initiates protein synthesis, provides methyl groups (as SAMe), and supports glutathione production.
Balance matters: adequate methionine supports detoxification, but very high intake without glycine may raise homocysteine.
Phenylalanine is an essential amino acid converted to tyrosine, which then becomes dopamine, norepinephrine, and epinephrine—key neurotransmitters for focus and mood.
Adequate in most protein-containing diets; people with PKU (phenylketonuria) must restrict it.
Threonine supports mucin production in the gut lining, collagen and elastin formation, and immune antibody production.
Found in most animal proteins and legumes; important for gut barrier integrity.
Tryptophan is the precursor to serotonin (mood, sleep) and niacin (NAD production)—making it uniquely important for mental health and energy.
Often the least abundant essential amino acid in food; turkey, dairy, and seeds are good sources.
Valine is a BCAA that supports muscle metabolism, tissue repair, and energy production during exercise.
Works alongside leucine and isoleucine; found in meat, dairy, legumes, and whole grains.
Arginine produces nitric oxide (vasodilation for blood flow), supports immune function, and aids wound healing—conditionally essential during growth or stress.
May support exercise performance via improved blood flow; found in nuts, seeds, and meat.
Cysteine is a sulfur-containing amino acid critical for glutathione synthesis (the body's master antioxidant), keratin (hair/nails), and detoxification.
Conditionally essential—synthesized from methionine but demand increases during illness, oxidative stress, or heavy training.
Glutamine is the most abundant amino acid in the body—fuels immune cells, maintains gut barrier integrity, and supports muscle recovery after intense exercise.
Conditionally essential during critical illness, surgery, or prolonged intense training when demand exceeds synthesis.
brainLongevity: After astrocytes take up glutamate, they convert it to glutamine and shuttle it back to neurons—glutamine is the central amino acid in the glutamate–glutamine cycle.

enough: Glycine supports collagen synthesis (skin, joints, tendons), glutathione production, creatine formation, and acts as an inhibitory neurotransmitter promoting sleep quality.
Research suggests 10+ g/day may improve sleep onset and collagen repair—far above typical dietary intake of 3–5 g.
brainLongevity: A glutathione precursor and inhibitory neurotransmitter—glycine helps astrocytes maintain antioxidant capacity and nervous-system calm alongside glutamate cleanup.

enough: Proline is a major component of collagen (makes up ~15% of its structure)—supporting skin elasticity, joint health, wound healing, and arterial integrity.
Conditionally essential during wound healing or intense collagen turnover; bone broth and gelatin are concentrated sources.
Tyrosine is the direct precursor to dopamine, norepinephrine, epinephrine, and thyroid hormones—conditionally essential during stress when demand outpaces synthesis from phenylalanine.
Supports mental performance under stress, cold, or sleep deprivation.
adhd: Tyrosine is the amino acid that tyrosine hydroxylase converts into L-DOPA—the immediate precursor to dopamine. When brain tyrosine availability drops (stress, poor sleep, stimulant medication), dopamine synthesis is directly rate-limited.

Protein-rich meals providing tyrosine support baseline dopamine production; some clinicians suggest tyrosine supplementation (500–2000 mg) for focus under acute stress, though long-term benefit over food sources is less clear.

enough: Taurine supports bile acid conjugation (fat digestion), heart muscle function, retinal health, and acts as an antioxidant in mitochondria.
Recent longevity research shows taurine declines with age and supplementation extended lifespan in animal models; human trials are ongoing.
antiAging: Taurine levels decline significantly with age; a 2023 Science study showed taurine supplementation extended lifespan in mice and improved healthspan markers (bone density, muscle, immune function).
Acts as a mitochondrial antioxidant and supports bile acid metabolism—both decline with aging. Shellfish, dark poultry meat, and fish are the richest food sources.
adhd: Taurine is the most abundant free amino acid in the brain and acts as a neuromodulator—binding glycine and GABA-A receptors to calm glutamate-driven excitability alongside dopamine and norepinephrine balance.

ADHD animal models (spontaneously hypertensive rats) show high-dose taurine can reduce hyperactivity and modulate striatal dopamine transporter (DAT) expression and dopamine uptake; low vs high doses can have opposite effects, so food-first intake matters.

Shellfish, dark poultry, beef, fish, eggs, and dairy are the practical food sources; human ADHD trials are still limited, but taurine is generally well tolerated and worth tracking when stimulant side effects or plant-heavy diets leave little from food.
brainLongevity: Binds GABA-A and glycine receptors to calm glutamate-driven excitability; also supports astrocyte and mitochondrial antioxidant defense.
